planting.note: Sow direct in spring or plant divisions in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; alpine rock cress is one of the finest early-spring rock garden plants, covering itself in pure-white (or pink in cultivars) flowers before most other plants have emerged.

directions: Sow direct in spring in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun, or plant divisions in spring or fall; alpine rock cress requires excellent drainage — plants will rot in wet or waterlogged soil especially in winter. Arabis alpina is a mat-forming European alpine perennial in the mustard family, producing rosettes of grayish-green, slightly hairy leaves and in early spring covering itself completely in pure-white, 4-petaled flowers in dense racemes that create a solid mat of white from March through May — one of the most reliable and spectacular early-spring groundcovers for rock gardens, walls, and sunny banks. The pink-flowered cultivar 'Rosea' and semi-double 'Flore Pleno' are popular alternatives; all are excellent for spilling over walls and between rocks. Shear lightly after blooming to keep compact and encourage fresh growth.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring or plant divisions in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; mountain alyssum is drought-tolerant and thrives in hot, dry rock gardens and on sunny, exposed banks.

directions: Sow direct in spring in very well-drained, lean, gritty, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; mountain alyssum is intolerant of shade and wet soil — it is a plant of hot, dry, rocky habitats and thrives on neglect and poor soil. Alyssum montanum is a low, mat-forming European perennial alyssum (not to be confused with the annual sweet alyssum, Lobularia maritima) with gray-green, densely hairy leaves and bright-yellow, honey-scented, 4-petaled flowers in dense, rounded clusters in early to mid-spring; the honey fragrance is less pronounced than sweet alyssum but still pleasant. It forms attractive, low mats on dry, rocky banks, in rock gardens, between paving stones, and on dry walls; it is drought-tolerant, long-lived, and essentially maintenance-free in appropriate conditions. More compact and lower-growing than gold dust alyssum (A. saxatile/Aurinia saxatilis).

planting.note: Sow direct in fall for spring germination, or sow in early spring; white campion self-seeds freely to naturalize and often behaves as a short-lived perennial or biennial in garden conditions.

directions: Sow direct in fall or early spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; white campion self-seeds freely and often naturalizes in gardens, roadsides, and meadows without intervention once established. Silene latifolia (syn. Melandrium album) is a common European short-lived perennial or biennial weedy wildflower naturalized throughout North America; it produces softly hairy, sticky stems bearing oval leaves and sweetly fragrant, pure-white, 5-petaled flowers with deeply notched petals that open in the evening and at night, making it particularly attractive to moth pollinators. The nocturnal fragrance is delightful in the summer garden; white campion is an important pollinator plant and naturalizes beautifully in wildflower meadows and cottage-garden borders. Male and female flowers are on separate plants (dioecious); both are needed for seed production.

planting.note: Plant divisions or sow seed in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline soil in full sun; sea campion is extremely salt- and wind-tolerant and thrives on coastal cliffs and rocky banks.

directions: Plant divisions or sow seed in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, gritty soil in full sun; sea campion requires superb drainage and tolerates no shade or waterlogging — a perfect rock garden and dry wall plant. Silene uniflora (syn. S. vulgaris subsp. maritima) is a native coastal European perennial forming compact, cushion-forming mats of blue-gray, glaucous leaves on cliffs, shingle beaches, and rocky coastal outcrops; it produces a profusion of white, deeply notched, 5-petaled flowers with distinctively inflated, bladder-like calyces (resembling small balloons below the petals) from late spring through summer. It is exceptionally salt- and wind-tolerant, making it valuable for coastal gardens, and forms an attractive, low groundcover on dry banks, in rock gardens, and on top of dry stone walls. The double-flowered cultivar 'Flore Pleno' is particularly ornamental.

planting.note: Plant divisions or sow seed in spring in very well-drained, alkaline to neutral, gritty soil in full sun; creeping baby's breath is a superb rock garden and dry wall plant, forming a dense mat that covers itself in tiny flowers for weeks in summer.

directions: Plant divisions or sow seed in spring in very well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean, gritty soil in full sun; creeping baby's breath requires excellent drainage and performs best in poor, slightly alkaline, gravelly soils similar to its native alpine and subalpine limestone habitats. Gypsophila repens is a low, mat-forming European alpine perennial producing slender, blue-gray leaves on spreading, prostrate stems and masses of tiny, white to pale-pink, 5-petaled flowers in loose panicles from early to mid-summer, creating a frothy, airy effect; it trails attractively over rocks and walls. Unlike the tall, annual or biennial florists' baby's breath (G. paniculata and G. elegans), this is a compact, perennial species ideal for rock gardens, dry walls, and the front of sunny borders. The pink-flowered selection 'Rosea' is particularly popular. Excellent for cutting and drying.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; rock soapwort is one of the most spectacular and vigorous rock garden and dry wall plants, spilling in cascades of bright-pink flowers.

directions: Sow direct in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; rock soapwort is an extremely vigorous spreader when well established — site it where it can spill freely over walls or banks without smothering smaller plants. Saponaria ocymoides is a trailing, mat-forming European alpine perennial in the pink family that completely covers itself with a stunning carpet of bright, deep-pink to rose-pink, 5-petaled flowers in late spring and early summer — one of the most floriferous of all rock garden plants, and one of the best for cascading over walls where it hangs in sheets of pink. The leaves contain saponin and produce a slight soapy lather when rubbed with water (giving the genus its name). After the first flowering, shear plants back hard to encourage a second flush and to keep the plant compact. One of the easiest and most rewarding alpine plants for sunny, dry sites.
